name: Lambertville Quarry
short_description: Historic quarry site near the Delaware River offering access to Ordovician-era limestone and dolostone formations. Rockhounders can find quartz, calcite, and feldspar specimens in weathered quarry ...
description:
Lambertville Quarry is a former limestone and dolostone quarry located in Delaware Township along the scenic Delaware River valley. The site exposes Ordovician-age carbonate rocks that have been quarried since the 19th century for construction aggregate and lime production. The quarry walls and surrounding tailings offer opportunities to collect quartz veins, calcite crystals, and feldspar specimens that formed within and weathered from the host rock.

The geology here reflects the ancient shallow marine conditions of the Ordovician Period, when this region was covered by a warm, shallow sea. The carbonate rocks contain fossil-bearing layers and occasional pockets of crystalline minerals deposited during diagenesis and later hydrothermal activity. This makes the site valuable for both mineral collectors and those interested in paleontology.

Access to the quarry depends on current ownership and operational status. Visitors should verify access conditions before planning a trip, as active quarries typically restrict public entry for safety reasons. When accessible, the gentle slope and exposed rock faces make this a moderate site for rockhounders with basic experience.

The Delaware River valley location provides easy access from nearby towns and scenic surroundings. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable collecting conditions, though the site can be visited year-round depending on weather and access permits.
history: Lambertville Quarry was developed in the 19th century as part of the industrial limestone and dolostone mining boom along the Delaware River valley. The Ordovician carbonate sequence exposed here (approximately 450 million years old) was deposited in a warm, shallow epicontinental sea and later uplifted during the Paleozoic mountain-building events that shaped eastern North America.
tips: Search the weathered quarry walls and fractured rock faces for quartz veins and secondary calcite crystals - these often line fractures and void spaces in the carbonate host rock. Bring a small pry bar or chisel to carefully extract specimens from loose blocks and talus. Look for translucent white or clear calcite clusters and milky quartz fragments in the upper weathered zone where acid-rich groundwater has dissolved surrounding carbonate matrix.
terrain_notes: Elevation of 81 feet with gentle slope terrain. The site features exposed quarry walls, talus fields, and broken rock fragments. Footing can be uneven due to loose rocks and rubble. Walking distance from parking is typically short but depends on access conditions. No maintained trail.
written_directions: From Lambertville, take Bridge Street east across the Delaware River into Pennsylvania briefly, then return via Route 32 north along the river. The quarry is located south of the town near river-level topography. Exact access roads depend on current property status - verify access before visiting as the site may be posted or actively worked.
road_conditions: Paved roads to general area; final approach may be unpaved or restricted depending on current operations
parking_notes: Parking availability depends on current quarry operations. Street parking or pullouts may be available near the site, but verify access conditions first.
collection_rules: Access to active quarry sites is typically restricted for safety reasons. Permission from the property owner or operator is required before collecting. Check current status and obtain written permission before visiting. Do not trespass on posted property. Wear appropriate safety gear including hard hat and eye protection if access is granted.
rules:
Obtain permission from property owner or operator before collecting
Wear hard hat and safety glasses if access is permitted
Do not enter if site is posted as restricted
No commercial collection without explicit permission
Respect all posted warnings and barriers
nearest_services: Lambertville, NJ - approximately 1 mile. Full services including gas, food, water, and restaurants available in town.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with loose talus and fractured stone fragments. Quarry walls present cliff hazard. Not wheelchair accessible. Sturdy footwear with good grip required. Moderate physical exertion needed to navigate talus slopes.

faq.question: Can I rockhound at Lambertville Quarry in New Jersey?
faq.answer: Lambertville Quarry is on private property and access depends on current ownership and operational status. You must obtain permission from the property owner or quarry operator before collecting any specimens. Contact local authorities or the quarry operator to verify access conditions before planning your visit.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can you find at Lambertville Quarry?
faq.answer: Rockhounders at Lambertville Quarry can find quartz veins, calcite crystals, and feldspar specimens. These minerals occur in fractures and void spaces within the Ordovician-age limestone and dolostone host rock. Calcite often forms clear to white clusters in weathered zones.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Lambertville, New Jersey?
faq.answer: March through May and September through November offer the most comfortable collecting conditions. Spring and fall provide mild temperatures and lower humidity compared to summer heat. Winter weather can make access difficult, and summer humidity may be uncomfortable for fieldwork.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect at Lambertville Quarry?
faq.answer: Since Lambertville Quarry is private property, you need explicit permission from the property owner or quarry operator - which functions as a permit requirement. There is no standard permit issued by the state for this site. Always verify current access status and obtain written permission before visiting.

faq.question: Is Lambertville Quarry beginner-friendly for rockhounding?
faq.answer: Lambertville Quarry is moderately difficult and better suited for rockhounders with some experience. The terrain features unstable talus, loose rocks, and quarry walls with cliff hazards. Beginners should seek easier, more accessible sites or visit with an experienced collector familiar with quarry safety.

faq.question: What tools and safety gear do I need at Lambertville Quarry?
faq.answer: Bring a small pry bar or chisel for specimen extraction, heavy gloves, sturdy boots with good traction, and a backpack. Essential safety equipment includes a hard hat and eye protection, as falling rock and unstable terrain pose hazards. Never enter without proper safety gear, especially in active quarry areas.

meta_description: Find quartz, calcite, and feldspar at Lambertville Quarry in New Jersey. Historic limestone quarry along Delaware River. Permission required.
